Press enter or click to view image in full size

As frontend applications continue to grow, managing hundreds of pages, shared components, API integrations, and multiple development teams becomes increasingly challenging. What starts as a simple project often evolves into a large codebase where every change impacts multiple features.
To address these challenges, I started building a Frontend Platform instead of another traditional Next.js application.
The objective was simple:

• Build reusable platform capabilities only once.

• Allow feature teams to develop modules independently.

• Keep modules isolated while sharing authentication, networking, permissions, and UI.

• Prepare the architecture for future migration to Micro Frontend.
The result is a modular architecture powered by Next.js, TypeScript, and pnpm Workspace.

Why Not a Traditional Next.js Project?
Most Next.js projects eventually end up looking like this:
app/
components/
hooks/
services/
utils/
pages/As the project grows, every feature shares the same folders.
After months or years, it becomes difficult to answer simple questions such as:

• Which files belong to the User feature?

• Which API is used only by Transfer?

• Can one team modify User without affecting Transfer?
This often leads to a tightly coupled codebase where features are no longer independent.

A Modular Approach
Instead of organizing the project by technical layers, every feature becomes its own package.
frontend-platform-poc/apps/
dashboard-shellpackages/
platform-core
platform-ui
module-user
module-transferEach module owns its pages, hooks, services, types, utilities, and components.
This separation improves ownership and reduces coupling between features.
The Dashboard Shell
The Dashboard Shell acts as the application’s entry point.
Its responsibilities are intentionally small:

• Render the application layout

• Handle navigation

• Load feature modules

• Share platform services
Business logic stays inside feature modules.
This allows new modules to be added without changing the shell architecture.
Platform Core
One of the primary goals was avoiding duplicated implementations across feature modules.
Instead of each module implementing its own authentication, HTTP client, or permission system, these responsibilities are centralized inside platform-core.
The package currently provides:

• Authentication Store

• HTTP Client

• API Layer

• Event Bus

• Permission Management

• Navigation Store

• Shared Types
Every module consumes the same platform services, ensuring consistency across the application.
Shared UI
Reusable UI components are extracted into platform-ui.
Instead of copying dialogs and layouts into every module, the platform exposes components such as:

• Header

• Sidebar

• Login Dialog

• Notification Center
This keeps feature modules focused on business functionality rather than application infrastructure.
Event-Driven Communication
One of the most interesting parts of the platform is the Event Bus.
Rather than importing modules directly, communication happens through events.
For example:
eventBus.emit("LOGIN", user);eventBus.emit("NOTIFICATION", {
type: "success",
message: "Transfer completed"
});Any module can subscribe without creating direct dependencies.
This pattern keeps modules loosely coupled and makes future migration to Micro Frontend much easier.
Dynamic Module Loading
Every feature exports a simple module manifest.
const module: PlatformModule = {
id: "user",
title: "User",
component: UserPage
};export default module;The Dashboard Shell dynamically renders modules without hardcoding every page.
Adding a new feature becomes significantly simpler because the shell doesn’t need to know how the module is implemented internally.
Module Generator
To keep every feature consistent, I also created a simple CLI.
Creating a new feature requires only one command.
pnpm create:module paymentThe generator automatically creates:

• package.json

• tsconfig.json

• folder structure

• module manifest

• page template

• barrel exports
This reduces repetitive work and ensures every module follows the same architecture.

Final Thoughts
Building a frontend platform requires more initial effort than starting with a traditional project structure. However, the benefits become increasingly valuable as the application and development team grow.
By separating platform capabilities from business features, introducing event-driven communication, and standardizing module creation, the architecture becomes easier to maintain, easier to scale, and better prepared for future evolution toward Micro Frontend.
The complete source code is available on GitHub, and future articles will dive deeper into the implementation of the Platform Core, Event Bus, Module Generator, and package publishing workflow.
